summary: Joel Spolsky argues that the best software developers are essentially never on the open job market because they get hired through connections, internships, and reputation before they ever need to send out resumes. This creates a paradox where traditional recruiting methods like job boards yield piles of resumes dominated by unqualified candidates, with very few worth considering. He proposes three strategies: going where great developers congregate (conferences, niche communities), building a robust internship pipeline that identifies talent before graduation, and creating a developer community around your company. He also warns against over-reliance on employee referrals, especially with cash incentives, which tend to lower hiring quality rather than improve it.
key_points: Great developers apply for roughly four jobs in their entire career and are almost never available on the open market, while unqualified candidates apply to hundreds or thousands of positions, creating a terrible signal-to-noise ratio in resume piles.

Recruiting should shift from 'gather and filter resumes' to 'track down winners and make them talk to you' by going to conferences, niche job boards, and places where talented developers already congregate.

Internship programs serve as extended auditions that let companies identify great talent before they enter the job market, justifying higher starting offers because the employer has better information about the candidate's abilities.

Employee referral bonuses create perverse incentives where employees coach friends through interviews and pressure hiring managers, ultimately lowering the quality of hires rather than improving them.
Building a developer community around your company creates a self-selecting audience of talented people you can recruit from whenever openings arise, though this strategy is difficult to replicate.
money_quotes: The great software developers, indeed, the best people in every field, are quite simply never on the market.
Instead of thinking as recruiting as a 'gather resumes, filter resumes' procedure, you're going to have to think of it as a 'track down the winners and make them talk to you' procedure.
The corollary of that rule—the rule that the great people are never on the market—is that the bad people—the seriously unqualified—are on the market quite a lot.
One good way to snag the great people who are never on the job market is to get them before they even realize there is a job market: when they're in college.

We've already auditioned these kids, and there's no risk that they won't be great. We know what they can do. So when we hire them, we have more information about them than any other employer who has only interviewed them.
I feel like I'm giving you advice that says, 'to win a beauty pageant, (a) get beautiful, and (b) enter the pageant.'
Nobody wants to persuade their friends to apply for a job at their company only to get rejected. It sort of puts a damper on the friendship.
Suddenly your entire workforce is trying to get you to hire someone's useless college roommate.
key_insight: The best developers never enter the job market, so effective recruiting means proactively finding them through conferences, internships, and community-building rather than passively filtering through resume piles.
